channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the Last Televised California Governor Debate
item.description: Candidates debated housing and insurance policy in the first half, then furiously attacked one another at the end.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Jon Rou/LMU
item.media:description: Seven candidates competing to be the next governor of California met for a debate at the Skirball Cultural Center in Los Angeles on Wednesday.

item.title: Judge Says F.B.I. Can Keep 2020 Election Records Seized From Georgia
item.description: The ruling is the latest in a saga driven by President Trump’s desire for redemption in the state, which he lost in 2020. The county is likely to appeal.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. searched an election center in Fulton County, Ga., in January.

item.title: Susan Collins Says She Has Long Had a Benign Tremor
item.description: The Republican senator from Maine, running for re-election at age 73 in one of this year’s top Senate races, made the disclosure after mounting online scrutiny on the left.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine, a Republican, is likely to face Graham Platner, a 41-year-old Democrat and a political newcomer, in the November general election.

item.title: Polls in California Governor’s Race Reflect a Chaotic Field
item.description: Four candidates have emerged as leaders in the primary election to succeed Gov. Gavin Newsom.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: A Times reporter in L.A. explains an unusual race.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators at the California Democratic Convention in February.

item.title: A Scaled-Back Celebration for Public Servants ‘in Trauma’
item.description: The Trump administration has eliminated more than 350,000 federal jobs. But an annual effort to inspire talented people to serve the public continues.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson
item.category: National Museum of the American Indian
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Samuel J. Heyman Service to America Medals are considered the government equivalent of an Oscar and go to civil servants nominated by their bosses and colleagues.

item.title: The sexual assault allegations against Eric Swalwell rattled the race.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín and Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Eric Swalwell at the California Democratic Convention in San Francisco in February.

item.title: Here’s what to know about the California governor race.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Antonio Villaraigosa, the former mayor of Los Angeles; Katie Porter, a former state representative; and the businessman Tom Steyer are among the candidates in a crowded field vying to become the next governor of California.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Pooja Salhotra

item.title: Trump’s Gyrations on the War Leave Even Rubio Out of Sync
item.description: The administration’s latest shifts on the status of the conflict show how treacherous it is to speak for a president who cultivates an erratic style.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio holding a briefing at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump’s Indiana Victory Shows His Enduring Grip on Core Republican Supporters
item.description: Low approval ratings? MAGA divisions? The president was able to turn out party loyalists in an Indiana primary to help him oust Republican state lawmakers who had crossed him.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Michigan’s Combative Democratic Senate Primary Heats Up
item.description: Attacks are flying, prominent Democrats are taking sides and the fight is just getting started.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Iran War Looms Over Vance as He Visits Iowa
item.description: The vice president, a skeptic of the war, made his first visit of the cycle to the state that will kick off the 2028 nominating process.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance visited Iowa on Tuesday and told an emotional story of meeting Gold Star families, including one that had lost a loved one in the war in Iran.

item.title: Justice Dept. Accuses U.C.L.A. Medical School of Bias Against White and Asian Applicants
item.description: The finding stems from a 2023 Supreme Court decision that overturned race-conscious admissions. U.C.L.A. officials said the school accepts students on merit.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Alisha Jucevic for The New York Times
item.media:description: The review of the U.C.L.A. medical school stems from a 2023 Supreme Court decision that overturned race-conscious admissions.

item.title: Jack Smith Calls the Justice Dept. ‘Corrupted’ by Trump and His Allies
item.description: At a private event in Washington last month, Mr. Smith, the former special counsel, accused Justice Department leaders of targeting people for prosecution to please and impress the president.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election Case)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Jack Smith, the special counsel who twice indicted President Trump, testifying at a hearing in January in Washington.

item.title: U.S. Troops in Middle East Wait for the Next Big Moment
item.description: The U.S. military is essentially on standby as officials give contradictory signals about the status of the Iran war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Getty Images
item.media:description: Soldiers of the 82nd Airborne Division at Fort Bragg, N.C., in 2021.

item.title: Jack Bass, Dean of South Carolina Political Journalism, Dies at 91
item.description: In articles and books, he wrote about the rapid racial, political and economic changes that transformed his state — and the South as a whole.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Alan Hawes/The Post and Courier, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jack Bass in 2005, the year he and Marilyn W. Thompson published “Strom: The Complicated Personal and Political Life of Strom Thurmond,” one of two biographies of Mr. Thurmond that they wrote together.

item.title: Suspect Michael Marx Charged With Shooting at Secret Service Agents Near National Mall
item.description: The suspect, a 45-year-old from Texas, faces three charges, including assaulting federal officers with a dangerous weapon.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Rod Lamkey/Associated Press
item.media:description: Police tape ringed the scene of the shooting in Washington on Monday.

item.title: How the Fight Over Israel Is Playing Out Inside MAGA
item.description: The war in Iran has added to a tectonic shift in public opinion — a bipartisan swing away from Israel. Some on the far-right are fighting to keep President Trump’s movement aligned with the Jewish state.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Laura Loomer, the far-right media figure, has emerged as one of the president’s most aggressive, pro-Israel enforcers.

item.title: Justice Department Sues Colorado Over Gun Law
item.description: The lawsuit, which follows a separate suit the agency filed against the city of Denver on Tuesday, involves a state ban on high-capacity ammunition magazines.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Rich Pedroncelli/Associated Press
item.media:description: A semiautomatic rifle with a high-capacity detachable magazine.

item.title: Office of Virginia State Senator Louise Lucas Searched by F.B.I. Agents
item.description: Two people familiar with the case said the search of a lawmaker’s business office was related to a Biden-era investigation of possible corruption and bribery related to marijuana dispensaries.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Ryan M. Kelly/Associated Press
item.media:description: State Senator L. Louise Lucas of Virginia, a Democrat, helped lead the push for a more aggressive congressional map meant to give her party 10 out of the state’s 11 seats in the U.S. House.

item.title: Conservative Group’s Connections at the State Department Raise Some Alarms
item.description: Founders of the Ben Franklin Fellowship are trying to dismantle pro-diversity practices in the agency and to boost career diplomats who promote President Trump’s ideas.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard for The New York Times
item.media:description: The State Department’s Harry S. Truman building in Washington.

item.title: Why Is Gas So Much More Expensive in Some States Than Others?
item.description: Shipping, taxes, competition and environmental regulations all play a part in the cost of gasoline. That can lead to stark differences in pump prices.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: The national average gas price has soared about 50 percent since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Howard Lutnick Grilled by Lawmakers Over Epstein Ties
item.description: The commerce secretary appeared for hours in a closed-door session on Wednesday with the House Oversight Committee
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Ana Swanson
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Capitol Hill in April. He is one of the highest-profile cabinet members to come under scrutiny in connection with Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: House Democrats’ Primary Endorsements Divide the Party
item.description: The Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee’s intervention in contested primaries in critical House races has highlighted deep divides over the party’s tactics and its future.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Jon Kopaloff/Getty Images
item.media:description: Randy Villegas, a candidate in California’s 22nd Congressional District, was among the Democrats in contested primaries who did not receive an endorsement from the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee.

item.title: A Look Inside the Case That Enshrined Political Power for Billionaires
item.description: After Watergate, Congress tried to curtail the role of money in politics. But a pivotal Supreme Court case nipped it in the bud. Years later, new details are emerging on how wealthy Americans were conferred with a “right to spend” on elections.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Keystone Press Agency/ZUMA Press Wire, via Reuters
item.media:description: A landmark Supreme Court case laid the groundwork for the tremendous amounts spent on today’s elections.
